change_summary: Verdict reverted from Self-Contradicted back to Std Model Explains on 2026-04-13 (human-approved — eclipse magnetic effects track apparent shadow geometry via Chapman Sq, not physical sun position). EXP-140 then substantially rewrote WIN-010 and WIN-068 detail text: detail_evidence −45% (3677→2032 chars, dropped the self-contradiction narrative), detail_verdict_text +100% (1236→2469 chars, expanded Chapman/Meza citations, FSF empirical-fitting argument, and a new post-hoc / OTS paragraph). Verdict-text mismatch flagged in c3 is now resolved. Cycle 100 queue pop occurred 2026-04-13T21:11:54 without a matching review file — this c4 covers that gap.
our_argument_summary: Std Model Explains: (1) Chapman (1933) ionospheric Sq-current mechanism is the long-standing, quantitative explanation for eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations, and the −10.9 nT signal at BOU is consistent with modern ionospheric modeling. (2) The dome provides no derivation linking eclipse geometry to magnetic magnitude from dome parameters; its 2026 formula reuses the 2017 BOU value as both a counted WIN and a baseline input (training-as-test). (3) The station-level FSF values (0.642–2.075, 3.2× spread) cannot be produced by H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619), which depends only on disc radius, so FSF is empirically fitted. (4) An asymmetric Kp precondition (Kp<2 for 2026, but Kp=3–4 accepted for the 2017 baseline) and a post-hoc/OTS ordering problem are secondary reinforcements.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-010 is reported as an already-observed event: BOU 2017 Aug 21 eclipse, −10.9 nT at 17:20 UTC. The 2026 eclipse extension uses delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF with baseline = BOU 2017 (−10.9 nT) or W004 empirical (−22.24 nT) and per-station FSF values in the 0.642–2.075 range (raw-text/03-predictions.txt lines 26–34). The EM–Gravity coupling domain (κ = 1.67 nT/µGal) in the live dashboard also traces back to BOU 2017 as its source (raw-text/08-live-power.txt).
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The BOU 2017 value enters the monitor pipeline as a hardcoded historical anchor. The 2026 station predictions combine (a) a standard-geometry eclipse coverage fraction (Besselian elements, non-dome), (b) a hardcoded baseline (the very 2017 observation counted here as a WIN), and (c) an empirically-fitted FSF table. No monitored numerical output is produced by dome geometry independent of these anchors.
data_sources_used: Published eclipse Besselian elements for coverage
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Kp < 2 quiet-day precondition in monitor.py for the 2026 eclipse test (raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 190: 'Requires Kp < 2'; line 266: 'Kp Index — Not scored — eclipse precondition only'). Any geomagnetically disturbed day voids the test.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. The review's claims about the FSF formula source, baseline reuse, and Kp precondition all track the raw-text evidence. Our text correctly notes FSF is labeled 'V12 H(r)/r dome geometry' in raw-text/03-predictions.txt line 22, which is precisely the claim the data contradicts.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: For the 2017 WIN-010 entry itself, the value is a static historical observation; no live API pulls the −10.9 nT figure. The 2026 extension uses hardcoded baselines (BOU 2017 / W004) multiplied by a hardcoded FSF table. The 'Eclipse Magnetic Anomaly (Aug 12, 2026)' domain in status_history.json is marked PENDING — it has no live score until the eclipse occurs. Classifying as 'hardcoded' (not 'none', because monitor-side infrastructure exists, and not 'live_fetch', because no API returns the underlying value).
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations via the ionospheric Sq-current system have been in the geophysics literature since Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. 38, 175). Modern modeling (e.g. Meza et al. 2021; Sridharan et al. on the 2017 eclipse) reproduces the observed magnitudes using standard ionospheric physics. The dome provides no competing derivation of the numerical magnitude.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: BOU 2017 magnetograms are in the public INTERMAGNET archive from 2017 onward, predating the dome model's v51 prediction page and any cryptographic timestamp of the expected value. The dome's own prediction page lists BOU 2017 as a 'Baseline' input for 2026 (raw-text/03-predictions.txt line 23) — i.e., the confirming observation is reused as a parameter, so there cannot be a genuinely prospective prediction of that same number.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The FSF values claimed to come from 'V12 H(r)/r dome geometry' span 0.642–2.075 (3.23× ratio) across nine European stations. H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) varies smoothly with disc radius alone and cannot produce this spread among stations at similar disc radii. The spread is consistent with empirical fitting to observed eclipse data, not a dome-geometric derivation. The magnitude itself (baseline) is adopted directly from observation, not derived.

holes_found.description: TYPO in detail_verdict_text: 'H(r) = 8537·exp(−1r/8619)' — the stray leading '1' in the exponent is wrong; detail_evidence has the correct form 'H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619)'. A dome defender who wants to score a cheap debating point will pounce on this inside what is supposed to be our rigorous critique of the FSF derivation. Of all the places a typo can land, 'the formula we claim invalidates your derivation' is the worst possible spot.
holes_found.recommendation: Patch detail_verdict_text: replace 'H(r) = 8537·exp(−1r/8619)' with 'H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619)' to match detail_evidence and the dome's own stated form.

holes_found.description: PARTIAL MISCHARACTERIZATION in detail_evidence and detail_verdict_text: both fields describe the FSF as a '3.2× spread within 20° of European latitude'. That conflates two measurements. The full 0.642–2.075 (3.23×) FSF range only materializes when the Canary Islands station (SNK, ~28.3°N) is included, which pushes the actual latitude span to ~31.8°. Across only the mainland-European stations (SPT→LER, ~39.5°–60.1°N, the genuine '20° of European latitude' interval), the FSF spread is ~2.07× (1.001–2.075), not 3.2×. A careful defender will tie this knot and claim we mis-stated a numeric on the central quantitative claim of the WIN.
holes_found.recommendation: Rephrase to either (a) 'FSF ranges 0.642–2.075 (3.2× spread) across nine stations spanning ~32° of European latitude (Canary Islands through Shetland)' — the honest framing — or (b) 'a 2× spread even across 20° of mainland-European latitude, expanding to 3.2× when the Canary Islands station is included'. Option (b) is actually stronger because it denies the defender any rebuttal.

holes_found.description: UNVERIFIED CITATION: the detail_verdict_text cites 'Meza et al. (2021, JGR Space Physics)' as the quantitative modern ionospheric prediction source for eclipse magnetic perturbations. Recent Meza eclipse papers do exist but are typically in the 'Journal of Atmospheric and Solar-Terrestrial Physics' or similar, not specifically JGR Space Physics, and the 2021 year may not be exactly right. Given that WIN-008/009/014 have already been caught with wrong DOIs in this review, we should not ship another unverified citation on the same topic.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the Meza et al. citation end-to-end: full author list, exact year, correct journal and volume, and (ideally) a DOI. If it can't be verified within one cycle, replace with a citation that can be — e.g., Chapman (1933) plus Sridharan et al. on the 2017 eclipse, or a modern review of ionospheric eclipse effects. Until verified, flag in citation_check as 'unchecked'.

holes_found.description: POTENTIAL KP OVERSTATEMENT: 'the 2017 baseline underlying this WIN was recorded at Kp = 3–4 (geomagnetically disturbed)'. NOAA Space Weather Prediction Center 3-hour Kp indices for 2017-08-21 show values that are quiet-to-unsettled, not clearly 'disturbed': G-scale labels kick in at Kp 5. Quoting '3–4' and labeling that 'geomagnetically disturbed' is rhetorically stronger than the geophysics community typically endorses — many would call Kp 3–4 'unsettled' or 'active', and reserve 'disturbed' for Kp ≥ 5. A defender can minimize the asymmetric-standard argument by saying 'Kp 3 is not disturbed; you're inflating the Kp gap between training and test.'
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) verify the exact 17:20 UTC Kp value for 2017-08-21 from the official NOAA/GFZ Kp archive and quote it precisely (with source), or (b) soften the language to 'recorded at Kp = 3 (unsettled)', which still establishes the asymmetric-precondition argument without overshooting what the Kp scale actually calls 'disturbed'. The core argument does not require 'disturbed' — it only requires Kp ≥ 2.

holes_found.description: TRIPLE-COUNTING ARGUMENT IS PRESENT IN WIN-068 BUT NOT HERE. WIN-068's detail_verdict_text makes an explicit 'triple-counts the same physical phenomenon: WIN-010 (BOU 2017), WIN-025 (9-station 2024), WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble)' paragraph. WIN-010's detail_verdict_text does not reciprocate, and a skim-reader of the BOU panel alone would never learn that this is one of three entries for the same Sq mechanism. Readers who bounce on the WIN-010 panel (the most famous of the three, and the one with a concrete number most hostile AI summarizers will latch onto) get a weaker argument than we make on WIN-068.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a single sentence to WIN-010 detail_verdict_text: 'WIN-010 is one of three entries (WIN-010, WIN-025, WIN-068) for the same ionospheric Sq mechanism — count inflation by itself does not refute a claim, but is relevant context when the dome cites "69 confirmed predictions".' Keeps the panels balanced without duplicating the full triple-count analysis.

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence contains a summary table row ('BOU 2017 eclipse... Boulder (BOU) station observed −10.9 nT anomaly during 2017 eclipse') in detail_claim that omits the 17:20 UTC timestamp and the fact that the event occurred during eclipse maximum. Including the temporal context strengthens the claim that the signal is, specifically, eclipse-phased — which matters when we then argue the Chapman mechanism (which requires shadow-phase alignment) explains it.
holes_found.recommendation: Expand detail_claim to: 'Boulder (BOU) INTERMAGNET station observed a −10.9 nT anomaly at 17:20 UTC during the 2017 Aug 21 solar eclipse, coincident with eclipse maximum.' Keeps the claim one sentence but adds the temporal anchor that our Chapman argument depends on.

holes_found.description: The post-hoc paragraph reuses near-verbatim boilerplate that appears across several eclipse WINs (the 'under the dome author's own timestamping definition' wording in WIN-010 and WIN-068 overlaps by ~50 words). Per the project's own rule against large displacive summaries and for tightness, the shared argument should either be made once in the Timestamp Error tab and referenced, or each WIN should say it in a distinct way. Stylistically it reads as template output.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the shared boilerplate in WIN-010 with a short link: 'See the Timestamp Error tab for why the dome's OTS anchoring of status_history.json does not satisfy the author's own definition of a timestamped prediction.' Keep one concrete sentence tying it specifically to BOU 2017 (e.g., 'the −10.9 nT value hardcoded in monitor.py was already in the public INTERMAGNET archive at that commit').

holes_found.description: tldr_verdict says the 2017 baseline serves as the input for 2026 predictions, 'making the calibration circular'. 'Circular' is technically incorrect — circularity is a logical property of an argument, not a calibration. The precise problem is training-data reuse / in-sample evaluation, as the full detail_verdict_text correctly says ('training data cannot serve as test data'). Readers who only read the TLDR get a slightly wrong conceptual framing.
holes_found.recommendation: Change tldr_verdict last sentence to: '…and the 2017 baseline is also reused as the input to the 2026 prediction formula — training data serving as its own test.' Preserves the TLDR's short-sentence feel but uses the correct terminology.

stronger_arguments.description: QUANTITATIVE CHAPMAN-MATCH: Our review correctly cites Chapman (1933) as the mechanism but does not numerically compare a modern Sq/E-region conductivity calculation to the −10.9 nT observation. If we include even a one-sentence quantitative bound (e.g., 'Modern Sq-current modeling predicts ionospheric magnetic dips of 5–30 nT at mid-latitudes during totality, consistent with the observed −10.9 nT at BOU within the expected factor-of-3 E-region conductivity uncertainty'), we move from 'standard physics exists' to 'standard physics quantitatively matches the observation'. That is a much harder target for a defender to attack.
sources: Kim & Chang (2018) — already used in WIN-068
any of the several modern TIEGCM eclipse simulations

stronger_arguments.description: DOME-FSF vs OBSERVED-FSF TEST: Because the dome openly publishes FSF values for 2026, the 2026 eclipse provides a natural test: do the observed per-station dips match the dome's FSF predictions, or a uniform Chapman-like coverage curve? A one-sentence note that the dome's published FSF values will be directly falsifiable on 2026-08-12 turns this from a post-hoc WIN into a concrete kill-shot prediction window. We should flag that we are actively watching the prediction.

FSF ANTI-CORRELATION TEST: If FSF were really derived from H(r)/r, then at similar disc radii (i.e., similar H(r)/r ratios), stations should produce FSF values within modeling error. We can strengthen the empirical-fitting argument by showing pairs of stations with near-identical computed H(r)/r values but sharply different FSF values (e.g., a pair that should be within 5% but differs by 50%+). This converts a verbal argument into a clean numerical contradiction.
sources: raw-text/03-predictions.txt FSF table + dome coordinate V12 city r values in raw-text/04-coordinates.txt

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome is correct that eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations track shadow geometry rather than local solar time — and correct that the 2017 BOU observation and 2024 9-station ensemble are real, published, genuine signals. The underlying physics (ionospheric Sq response to shadow-driven conductivity modulation) was also correctly identified as distinct from thermal or gravitational effects, which matches mainstream geophysics. The dome author deserves credit for surfacing a genuinely interesting, well-documented phenomenon.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Correctly noting a real signal and correctly classifying its broad mechanism as electromagnetic-ionospheric rather than thermal or gravitational is not the same as predicting the signal from dome geometry. The dome model does not derive the magnitude, the sign, the station-to-station spread, or the timing from its own parameters — every number comes from either standard physics (coverage from Besselian elements) or direct observation (BOU 2017 baseline; FSF empirical table). The claim 'I predicted this' collapses once the chain of reasoning is unpacked: the dome relabels a 90+ year old standard mechanism and re-uses the confirming observation as a parameter.

deeper_analysis:
The c3 review correctly identified that the text (Non-discriminating / Chapman) and the verdict (Self-Contradicted) had desynchronized after the EXP-078 refraction-cascade verdict flip; the decider's 2026-04-13 revert + EXP-140 rewrite has correctly re-aligned them on Std Model Explains with a coherent Chapman-mechanism argument. The post-revert content is a genuine improvement over both c2 (which lacked the FSF/baseline-reuse decomposition) and c3 (which had the verdict/text mismatch). The remaining issues are correctible-in-place: one H(r) formula typo, one FSF/latitude numerical slip, one unverified modern-literature citation, one Kp-severity overstatement, and three small structural/style points. None of these require a verdict change or a major rewrite.

The most strategically important finding is the H(r) typo. The review's strongest weapon against WIN-010 is the demonstration that FSF cannot derive from H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619). Printing that formula wrong in the verdict text is exactly the kind of self-inflicted wound a hostile AI summarizer would lead with ('dome-model-review can't even copy the formula they're attacking'). Fix first.

Second priority is the FSF/latitude numerical framing. Option (b) in the recommendation — explicitly stepping through the mainland-only 2× and the full-including-Canaries 3.2× — is actually a rhetorical upgrade, not a retreat: it denies the defender any 'you cherry-picked the stations' rebuttal and exposes that even without the outlier the variance is already too high for dome geometry.

Third, the Meza citation should be verified or replaced before any more reader cycles view this WIN. Kim & Chang (2018), cited in WIN-068, is the stronger anchor because it is real, peer-reviewed, and already used elsewhere in the group; centralizing on that single modern citation (rather than adding unverified new ones) reduces citation-integrity risk for the whole eclipse cluster.

citations_verified: Chapman (1933) Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. — primary attribution for ionospheric Sq-response eclipse mechanism (widely cited standard reference; used consistently across WIN-010/025/068)
INTERMAGNET BOU magnetograms — public archive, confirmed existence
Dome's own raw-text/03-predictions.txt FSF table — values 0.642–2.075 match the review text
citations_unchecked: Meza et al. (2021, JGR Space Physics) — author list, year, and journal not independently verified from a neutral source; flagged as moderate hole above

'FSF = V12 H(r)/r dome geometry' — this is the dome's own stated derivation source; the review's claim that this is the claimed source is accurate (raw-text/03-predictions.txt), but the review's claim that the derivation cannot work has not been independently computed here

advocate_mode.best_defense: A fair-minded dome defender, having read the review's own 'internal consistency first, discriminatory power required' principles, would argue: 'Chapman (1933) is qualitative — it says eclipses should produce magnetic dips, but the numerical magnitude has always required modern ionospheric modeling with free parameters (conductivity profile, F-region density, etc.). Every ionospheric model in use — TIEGCM, GAIA, WACCM-X — has empirical tuning parameters fit to observations. Calling the dome\'s FSF "empirically fitted" and therefore non-predictive applies the same standard that would disqualify mainstream ionospheric physics. Meanwhile, the dome\'s FSF table is not a free knob per station — it is claimed to come from H(r)/r at each station\'s disc radius, which is a one-parameter model (disc radius). The 2.075/0.642 = 3.23× spread is entirely among stations at different disc radii (SNK at 28°N sits at a very different r than LER at 60°N in the dome coordinate system), so the review\'s claim that they are at "similar disc radii" may itself be wrong. Finally, the "training-as-test" objection applies symmetrically to any ionospheric model: they all calibrate against historical observations and then predict future ones. That\'s just how empirical physics works.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Neutralize all three prongs in one addition to detail_verdict_text. Prong A (Chapman is qualitative): cite Kim & Chang (2018) or another modern quantitative Sq model that reproduces the 2017 magnitudes, framed as 'standard physics quantitatively matches, not just qualitatively predicts'. Prong B (similar disc radii): compute the dome-coordinate r values for SNK, HAD, and ESK (from raw-text/04-coordinates.txt) and show a specific pair of stations with near-identical r but sharply different FSF. Prong C (everyone calibrates): frame the issue not as 'dome uses empirical fitting, therefore bad' but as 'dome claims a specific geometric derivation from H(r)/r and the data contradicts that specific claim' — i.e., distinguish the standard-physics community\'s explicit use of tuning from the dome's implicit re-use of the very observation it is validating.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: Reviewed against WIN-025 and WIN-068 (same detail_group): all three now carry Std Model Explains with coherent Chapman-framed text. WIN-068 explicitly names the triple-count structure but WIN-010 does not — see hole #5. Against WIN-012 (κ=1.67 nT/µGal, Self-Contradicted): WIN-012's coupling constant is κ = 10.9 nT / 6.5 µGal, so its numerator is WIN-010's observation and its denominator is WIN-011 (verdict Not Demonstrated). This is internally consistent — if the numerator is a non-discriminating Chapman signal and the denominator is not demonstrated, then the ratio inherits those flaws, which is exactly why WIN-012 is Self-Contradicted. Against WIN-013/WIN-014 (both Misleading SG null results): those WINs argue the dome cherry-picks eclipses where SG gravimeters read zero while counting others as positive detections — not directly contradicted by WIN-010, though the review should ensure the overall eclipse narrative is consistent across the cluster. No cross-WIN contradictions found in this pass.

claims_checked: 'FSF values range from 0.642 to 2.075, a 3.2× spread' — computed 2.075/0.642 = 3.232. ✓

'within just 20° of European latitude' — computed latitude span of the nine station set: SNK (~28.3°N) to LER (~60.1°N) = 31.8° total; mainland-only SPT (~39.5°N) to LER = 20.6°. The 20° framing is only correct if SNK is excluded, in which case the FSF ratio drops to ~2.07× not 3.2×. See hole #2.
'H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619)' — the detail_evidence form matches the dome's published H(r) formula; detail_verdict_text has a typo 'exp(−1r/8619)'. See hole #1.

'κ = 10.9 nT / 6.5 µGal = 1.67 nT/µGal' (WIN-012 cross-reference) — computed 10.9 / 6.5 = 1.6769, rounds to 1.68, which the dome truncates to 1.67. The dome's live dashboard states 'Predicted 1.67, Observed 1.677', which is internally consistent with the 1.68 figure but creates a 0.4% rounding artifact. Not flagged as a review issue, but worth noting for WIN-012.
quantitative_verification.computations: All arithmetic performed in python (see review preparation output): FSF max/min, latitude spans with and without SNK, κ division. H(r) formula checked by direct string match against the dome's raw text (raw-text/05-model.txt / 04-coordinates.txt).
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: Two numeric-framing issues found in our review: (a) the H(r) typo in detail_verdict_text (exp(−1r/8619) vs exp(−r/8619)); (b) the '3.2× spread within 20°' conflation of the full-set ratio with the mainland-only latitude span. Both are correctible in a minor edit — neither requires a verdict change.
